title: When the Discriminator Gets Too Good
concept: If the discriminator becomes near-perfect at telling real from fake early in training, it starts handing back extremely confident, near-certain verdicts instead of nuanced ones — and that overconfidence is where the next GAN training struggle begins.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is mode collapse, the failure mode covered just before this?

options.text: The generator finds a narrow set of outputs that reliably fool the discriminator and keeps repeating them instead of producing varied results
The discriminator refuses to output any judgment at all
The generator and discriminator both stop training permanently

exercises.explain: Right — mode collapse is the generator settling for a narrow winning trick instead of variety. Today: a completely different way GAN training can quietly break.

cards.title: The judge who's seen it all
cards.body: Imagine a talent-show judge who has watched ten thousand auditions. By the third second of any act, they already know — no hesitation, no maybe. Early in GAN training, the discriminator can reach that same instant-certainty stage long before the generator has learned anything good.

cards.title: A discriminator that stops hedging its bets
cards.body: If the discriminator learns much faster than the generator early on, it can quickly become highly accurate at spotting fakes. Instead of outputting cautious, in-between scores, it starts issuing extremely confident verdicts — close to 0 ("definitely fake") or 1 ("definitely real") — for nearly everything it sees.

cards.title: The instant-verdict art appraiser
cards.body: Picture an art appraiser who, after decades on the job, can spot a forgery the moment it enters the room — no squinting, no second look, just an immediate "fake" stamped with total certainty. That's useful for the appraiser's own accuracy, but a forger standing nearby learns nothing about which brushstroke gave them away.

cards.title: What the numbers start to look like
cards.body: Early in training, a healthy discriminator might score a fake image 0.55 (barely leaning "fake") — mildly unsure. An overconfident discriminator instead scores that same fake 0.02 — essentially certain, no matter how close the fake actually came to fooling it.

cards.title: Isn't a highly accurate discriminator good news?
cards.body: You might assume a discriminator that's very good at its job is purely a win — better judging should mean better training. In reality, that extreme confidence is exactly where trouble starts: the more certain its verdicts become, the less useful they turn out to be for teaching the generator anything. Next lesson digs into why.

cards.title: Uncertain vs. overconfident discriminator
cards.body: How the discriminator's scores change as it gets ahead early:

left.text:
Scores cluster near 0.5
Verdicts feel tentative
Plenty of room to nudge

right.text:
Scores sit near 0 or 1
Verdicts feel absolute
Little room left to nudge

exercises.prompt: Why might the discriminator become overconfident early in GAN training?

options.text: It can learn to spot the generator's still-weak fakes faster than the generator can learn to improve them
It is programmed with a hard rule to always output 0 or 1
The generator asks it to be more confident
It runs out of training data to judge

exercises.explain: Exactly — when the discriminator's learning outpaces the generator's early on, its judgments quickly become extreme and confident.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An overconfident discriminator outputs scores close to 0.5, showing it's still unsure.
exercises.explain: False. An overconfident discriminator does the opposite — its scores push toward the extremes, near 0 or 1, not toward the uncertain middle.

exercises.sentence: When the discriminator gets far ahead early in training, its judgments become extremely ___ instead of cautious and in-between.
exercises.explain: An overpowered discriminator stops hedging — its verdicts become extremely confident, near 0 or 1.

exercises.prompt: Which discriminator output shows the overconfidence problem building?

options.text: A generated fake image is scored 0.01 — the discriminator is nearly certain it's fake
A generated fake image is scored 0.48 — the discriminator is nearly torn
A generated fake image is scored 0.52 — the discriminator leans only slightly toward real

exercises.explain: A score pinned near 0 (or near 1) — not the uncertain middle — is the signature of an overconfident discriminator.

exercises.prompt: The discriminator's judgments have become extremely confident, near 0 or 1, instead of cautious. What's the natural next question?

options.text: What happens to the training signal sent back to the generator when the discriminator's output gets pinned at those extremes?
How many pixels does a generated image need?
What color scheme looks best in a user interface?
How long should a training dataset be stored on disk?

exercises.explain: Exactly! Up next: what happens to the feedback signal itself once the discriminator's confidence maxes out.
